calendar.matters:
MASTER CALENDAR COURT
Off Calendar Feb-11-2020 Vacated

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT And Or Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Jan-07-2020 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Stephen Preonas, Esq. of KATZOFF & RIGGS LLP, T:310.226.7843, for defendant/moving party, on Court Call;
No other appearance.

RULING: There being no objection, the Court adopted its tentative ruling on DEFENDANT EMMANUEL LIOTARD-VOGT's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT And Or Summary Adjudication, as follows:

Defendant's motion for summary judgment is granted. It is undisputed that the parties entered into a partnership at will within the meaning of the California Revised Uniform Partnership Act (RUPA). Corp. Code 16101(11). Dissociation occurs upon "[t]he partnership's having notice of the partner's express will to withdraw as a partner or on a later date specified by the partner." (Id. 16601(1).) Partnership dissolution occurs "[i]n a partnership at will, by the express will to dissolve and wind up the partnership business of at least half of the partners, including partners . . . who have dissociated within the preceding 90 days, and for which purpose a dissociation under paragraph (1) of Section 16601 constitutes an expression of that partner's will to dissolve and wind up the partnership business." (Id. 16801(1).)

Defendant meets his burden to show that there is no triable issue that he dissociated from the partnership prior to November 30, 2015, thereby dissolving the partnership. In response to requests for admission, Plaintiff expressly admitted that prior to November 30, 2015, Defendant withdrew from the partnership, that Defendant informed Plaintiff of his desire to withdraw from the partnership, and that Plaintiff had notice of Defendant's express will to withdraw. (Preonas Decl., Ex. A; see also Liotard-Vogt Decl. 6-9.) Further, Plaintiff's own actions show that he understood the partnership was dissolved. For example, in his November 2, 2015 response to Defendant's dissociation email, Plaintiff stated that he had suspended all partner draws, he asked Defendant immediately to assume responsibility for payment of his mobile phone account, and he estimated Defendant's "share of close down cost," making it unmistakably clear that he understood that the partnership had dissolved and would have to be wound up. (Liotard-Vogt Decl., Ex. A.) Likewise, on November 29, 2015, Plaintiff posted a message to the restaurant's customers informing them-utilizing the past tense-that Defendant "has decided to leave Pomelo." (Preonas Decl., Ex. A.) That Defendant worked shifts on December 1 and 2, 2015 without being paid wages does not raise any triable issue of material fact as to the date on which the partnership was dissolved; it merely illustrates that the partnership's business continued during the windup phase.

In view of these undisputed facts, plaintiff's first cause of action for dissolution of partnership is moot. It is also barred by the two-year statute of limitations that governs claims based upon an oral partnership agreement, as is the second cause of action for an accounting and the third cause of action for declaratory relief, which is merely derivative of the second cause of action.

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com
